retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews and compares the indications for each procedure and provides a detailed discussion of the surgical techniques. The relative benefits of radical orchiectomy and testis-sparing surgery in dealing with small, solid testicular neoplasms are compared. This activity highlights the critical role of the urologist in evaluating the relative benefits of each procedure and treating patients who undergo these surgeries. This activity also covers the indications, contraindications, complications, and management of radical orchiectomy and testis-sparing surgical procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Despite clear and growing indications as well as recommendations in published American Urological Association and European Association of Urology guidelines, testis-sparing surgery is still performed infrequently. Factors limiting its widespread use include inadequate training and experience with the technique among most practicing urologists, lack of confidence in intraoperative frozen section analysis, unavailability of an experienced and trusted genitourinary pathologist, a recommendation that these complex procedures be performed in high-volume tertiary care centers of excellence, and the requirement for intraoperative ultrasonography and surgical microscopy skills for impalpable lesions. [4] [7] [223] [229]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Radical orchiectomy is not only a diagnostic procedure but also a therapeutic one in patients with suspected testicular malignancies, whereas testis-sparing surgery is increasingly used for small testicular neoplasms in selected cases when there is a high likelihood of a benign histology.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Histological reports indicate that about two-thirds of patients with solid testicular masses ≤2.5 cm and negative serum tumor markers have benign lesions. [8] These findings suggest that testis-sparing surgery can be performed more frequently to avoid many unnecessary orchiectomies.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Testis-sparing surgery is a reasonable option for males with small, solid testicular neoplasms, as frozen section procedure is highly reliable in detecting malignancies intraoperatively, conversion to a radical orchiectomy does not appear to negatively affect oncological outcomes, and appropriate adjuvant treatment (radiotherapy) provides excellent control if germ cell CIS is detected in the residual parenchymal biopsies. [195] [206]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 593
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE GENITOURINARY SYSTEM is organized under two major categories: cancer of other male genital organs and cancer of the testis. Within the hierarchy of male genital neoplasms, cancer of other male genital organs serves as the parent category for penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي), while cancer of the testis is the parent category for testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية). Additionally, the broader entity Disorder of male reproductive system is classified as a subcategory of testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية). This hierarchical arrangement links the clinical classification of genitourinary neoplasms to specific organ‑specific cancers, clarifying how penile and testicular malignancies fit within the overall taxonomy of male reproductive system disorders and supporting more precise diagnosis and management.
